No selection -> returns False so the character types normally. Unlike _wrap_selection (Ctrl+B/I/E) there is no unwrap toggle — typing a bracket again nests, which is the expected editor behaviour. Tests: TestMarkdownFormatting bracket-surrounds/closing-bracket-and-quote/no-selection-types-normally/reversed-selection + key-controller-runs-at-capture. README notes bullet + shortcuts table updated. GOTCHA (first release was broken for real typing): GtkTextView's built-in input-method key controller runs in the CAPTURE phase and consumes printable keys there, so a bubble-phase EventControllerKey only ever receives modifier combos (which is why Ctrl+B worked but plain brackets never arrived). Fix: our key controller is now capture-phase too; being prepended it runs before the IM, and returning False for unhandled keys lets them type normally. Direct _on_key-call tests cannot catch this class of bug — the regression test asserts the controller phase/order instead.

column2: Text-first scratchpad on startup
column5: DONE (2026-07): launching Sidemark with no file now opens the persistent scratchpad as a TEXT-FIRST page at ~/.local/share/sidemark/scratchpad.md (ink in scratchpad-ink.json) instead of the old blank scratchpad.pdf. _open_scratchpad creates an empty .md and routes through _do_open_file -> _open_markdown, so the sheet gets the full text-first treatment (verbatim round-trip, ink sidecar, chronological undo); title stays "Scratchpad". The recents filter in _remember_recent now skips both scratchpad.md and scratchpad.pdf. The old scratchpad.pdf is left on disk and can still be opened manually. Test: TestTextFirstMode scratchpad-opens-as-a-text-page (HOME redirected to a tempdir; asserts text mode, file creation, clean dirty flag, recents exemption). README: text-first bullet mentions the startup scratchpad.

column2: Text page tools: caret tool, Alt+drag pen, PDF-only menus gated
column5: DONE (2026-07): on a text-first page the PDF select tool swaps for a dedicated caret tool - leftmost in the strip and preselected, drawn as a cairo I-beam glyph (_draw_mode_text) so the text page reads 'just type' by default. It maps to the same internal 'select' mode; _set_tool_mode routes the active state to the caret button while _text_mode. Alt+drag draws with the pen without switching tools: a capture-phase GestureDrag on the TextPageView overlay claims the sequence when Alt is held and denies itself otherwise, so clicks/selection are untouched; the pen style is borrowed for the stroke and the tool restored on release. The long-press tool variant menus (reading-order/rectangular select, free-hand/text highlighter) describe PDF behaviours and stay shut on a text page (_tool_style_popup guard). The Alt modifier hint lights the pen button in text mode. Tests: TestTextFirstMode alt-drag-draws-with-pen / tool-style-menus-stay-shut + updated chrome tests.

column2: Export a text page to PDF (text + ink, A4 pagination)
column5: DONE (2026-07): 'Export as PDF...' in the hamburger menu (text pages only; the inverse of the PDF-only group). _write_text_pdf snapshots the sheet widget at 2x via Gtk.WidgetPaintable -> render_texture per page slice, pastes the raster into a cairo.PDFSurface (A4, sheet width = page width) and draws the ink on top as vectors (clipped per page, so strokes spanning a break appear on both). Page breaks land on display-line tops (TextPageView.page_break_offsets), falling back to a hard cut only for an over-tall line. Raster keeps the export byte-exact with the screen (same GTK rendering); a selectable-text vector export would need a parallel Pango renderer - possible later refinement. Test: TestTextFirstMode export-writes-a4-pages (page count, A4 size, content on every page - also guards against GtkTextView culling offscreen lines from the snapshot).

column2: Sheet zoom for text pages (paper, text and ink together)
column5: DONE (2026-07): Ctrl+scroll / Ctrl+= / Ctrl+- on the sheet zoom the whole paper, Ctrl+0 resets (TextPageView.set_zoom / zoom_step, 0.5x-3x, multiplicative 1.1 steps). Width, inner margins and font-size all scale by the same factor - the text wraps at the same words, so the mark-anchored ink stays glued (effective font_px = base x zoom feeds the existing stroke-scaling machinery). Font CSS via a per-instance display provider scoped by a unique css class (present-bar pattern). The sheet's Ctrl+scroll no longer changes the persistent notes-font setting - zoom is per page view, the panel font stays a separate knob. Test: TestTextFirstMode sheet-zoom-scales-paper-text-and-ink.

column2: Text-snapping highlighter on text pages
column5: On a text-first page, highlighter strokes should snap to the line of text under them - straighten to the baseline band, extend to word boundaries - like marking prose with a real marker. The PDF side already has a 'mark text' highlight style; this is its text-page counterpart, anchored to the same GtkTextMarks as free-hand ink so it reflows with edits.

column2: Pagination / print view for text pages
column5: Optional page breaks at A4 height instead of one endless sheet, so a note previews like the printable document it becomes on export. The export already computes display-line page breaks (TextPageView.page_break_offsets); a print view would draw the same boundaries as gaps/shadows between paper sections and keep ink anchored across them.

column2: Margin inks: anchor without horizontal reflow
column5: Strokes drawn in the sheet margins (left of ~x=56 or right of the text column) should anchor to the nearest line vertically but keep their absolute x, so margin notes and brackets don't slide when the line's text changes. Today every stroke stores x offsets relative to the anchor character and scales with the font, which is right inside the text column but wrong for marginalia.

column2: Lasso re-anchoring for text-page ink
column5: DONE (2026-07) as part of #108 (text/PDF parity items 1-3). As predicted here: the PDF lasso is the selection model, and the text-page twist is re-anchoring — TextPageView._reanchor_selected() gives each moved/resized stroke a fresh left-gravity mark at its new first point and recomputes the per-point offsets at the current font, so the drawing rides with the paragraph it now sits on. Sidecar hash lines need no special handling: ink_to_json() reads the (new) mark, so the next save serialises the new anchor naturally. See #108 for the full notes.

column2: Code spans render verbatim (no LaTeX/scripts/bold inside `code`)
column5: DONE (2026-07): inline `code` spans are now literal in every render path - the notes editor, callout/text-box Pango markup and the PDF/marks export. A shared _split_code_spans() chops a line into (segment, is_code) runs (_MD_CODE_SPAN_RE = r'`[^`\n]+?`'); _symbolize skips code segments, _notes_to_pango_markup emits <tt>escaped</tt> for them with nothing else applied, and MarkdownNotesView._highlight_line skips bold/italic and ^/_ super/subscript that fall inside a code range. Fixes plain-Markdown files (helloworld.md) where `snake_case`, `2^10` or `\alpha` inside backticks were being re-rendered. Tests: TestLatexFormatting code-span cases, TestCalloutMarkup.test_code_span_is_verbatim et al.

column2: Per-version single-instance id (dev checkout != installed copy)
column5: DONE (2026-07): _application_id() scopes the GApplication id to the copy of the code being run so launching a working-tree checkout for a smoke test no longer forwards into (and vanishes behind) the installed instance the user is using. Installed paths (/usr/share/sidemark/sidemark.py, ~/.local/share/sidemark/sidemark.py) keep the canonical de.hspitz.sidemark (so the window still matches the .desktop icon and all launches share one instance for tab-dragging); any other path gets de.hspitz.sidemark.v<hash-of-realpath>. SIDEMARK_INSTANCE=<name> forces a suffix on purpose (empty -> base). SIDEMARK_STANDALONE (Ctrl+R reload) still adds NON_UNIQUE. Tests: TestInstanceId.

column2: Open launched files as a tab in the last-used window (fewer windows)
column5: DONE (2026-07): a file opened from outside a window (file manager, `sidemark file.pdf`, a second launch) now lands as a tab in the window you were last using instead of spawning a new one. PDFEditorApp._open_target() picks a reuse target via _reuse_target() (get_active_window(), which tracks focus order - the closest a Wayland client gets to 'the window on my current screen' - falling back to any open PDFEditorWindow) and calls open_file_in_tab()+present(); with no editor window open, or SIDEMARK_NEW_WINDOW=1, it falls back to a fresh window. do_command_line's shell note says whether it reused the current window or made a new one. Wayland hides window position/monitor/workspace from clients, so 'current screen' is approximated by focus order. Tests: TestOpenTargetReuse.

column2: Wiki-style [[links]] in notes: jump between slides / documents
column5: DONE (2026-07): [[wiki links]] in the notes navigate in-app - the lecture use case of pointing from one slide to another that explains it. Syntax (parsed by _parse_note_link): [[#page=N]] / [[#N]] (same document), [[file.pdf]], [[file.pdf#page=N]] / [[#N]]; paths relative to the current doc. In MarkdownNotesView they render via the 'link' TextTag (underlined, coloured) with the [[ ]] hidden off the cursor line; Ctrl+click follows (_on_link_click -> on_follow_link -> PDFEditorWindow._follow_note_link), hover shows a hand cursor. Same-doc links call _go_to_page; file links open_file_in_tab()+jump (reusing the current tab if it's already that doc). A new _split_markup() (kind text/code/link) and _MD_LINK_RE (negative lookbehind leaves the ![[embed]] marker alone) keep link targets verbatim everywhere - _symbolize skips them, _notes_to_pango_markup renders them as <u>label</u> in callouts, and _highlight_line suppresses ^/_ and bold inside them. Round-trips through Obsidian (same syntax). Follow-up (idea): bake into real GoTo/GoToR annotations on export so links work in any viewer. Tests: TestNoteLinkParse, TestFollowNoteLink, TestCalloutMarkup/TestLatexFormatting link cases.

column2: Wiki-link authoring ergonomics (aliases + autocomplete + link-to-here + backlinks)
column5: Follow-up to row 99: make [[links]] pleasant to AUTHOR, not just to follow. Page-level targets are enough for now (keep target strings extensible for a future #anchor). Execution order & full handoff in notes/linking-roadmap.md. (1) DONE Display aliases [[target|label]]: _parse_note_link splits on the first '|' (target drives path/page, right side is the label); _highlight_line hides the 'target|' prefix off the cursor line and styles only the label (whole span stays clickable); _notes_to_pango_markup shows the alias in callouts for free. Obsidian-compatible. Tests: TestNoteLinkParse alias cases, TestLatexFormatting.test_wiki_link_alias_hides_target_shows_label, TestCalloutMarkup.test_wiki_link_alias_shows_label_not_target. (2) DONE autocomplete popup on '[[': typing [[ opens a custom Gtk.Popover (autohide=False so the notes view keeps focus; nav keys intercepted in MarkdownNotesView._on_key while visible). Candidates from PDFEditorWindow._link_completions = open tabs + recent files + a 'this page' entry, minus the current doc; pure ranking/filter in module fns _link_query_at_cursor / _link_candidates / _link_insert_path (relative path, same-folder=basename). Enter/Tab inserts + closes the ]] (_insert_link_target), Up/Down move, Esc dismiss. Show is gated on view.get_mapped() (a Popover needs a realized surface) so a hidden notes panel / headless tests don't crash - acceptance still works off _link_rows. Tests: TestLinkAutocompleteHelpers, TestLinkAutocompletePopup, TestLinkCompletions. (3) TODO link-to-here capture (shortcut yields [[thisfile#page=N]] to clipboard/cursor). (4) TODO backlinks (index .md sidecars for 'what links here'). Verified while planning: follow-then-jump has no async race (canvas.load is synchronous, a fresh non-dirty tab is created first); cross-window dedup is a deferred minor polish.

column2: Autosave & crash recovery for text-first pages
column5: DONE (2026-07): _autosave_tick required s._path, but a text page's document IS its .md (_path=None) — so the mode built for taking lecture notes was exactly the one with no crash safety net. The tick now snapshots dirty text sessions keyed by _notes_path via _write_text_autosave_for (note.md + ink.json + meta.json in the same _autosave_dir_for hash dir; distinct filenames so a PDF snapshot for the same path can't be mistaken for a text one — _find_text_autosave checks note.md, _find_autosave checks doc.pdf). _open_markdown offers recovery via _maybe_offer_text_recovery (restores buffer text + ink from the snapshot, marks dirty so the user still decides whether it lands on disk). Snapshots are discarded on save (_on_save) and on explicit Discard in the tab-close and window-close prompts. An UNTITLED page has no path to key a snapshot by and stays uncovered until save-as names it (documented in the README autosave section). Tests: TestAutosave._find_text_autosave cases (newer/stale/pdf-snapshot-mismatch) + test_text_page_autosave_tick_and_cleanup_on_save.

column2: Fix batch: lasso vs straight lines / link-follow crash / CSS provider leak
column5: DONE (2026-07), three fixes from a review pass. (1) _finish_lasso selected only strokes with a POINT inside the loop; a snapped straight line is stored as exactly 2 points, so lassoing its middle caught nothing — contradicting the README's 'any stroke touching the loop is caught'. New _segments_intersect / _polyline_crosses_polygon: a stroke is selected if any point is inside OR any segment crosses the loop boundary. (2) _follow_note_link: os.path.samefile(full, self._path) raised FileNotFoundError when the OPEN document had been deleted/renamed on disk (the link target is isfile-checked; the current path wasn't) — Ctrl+click on a link tracebacked. Now caught (OSError → treat as a different document and open it). (3) Gtk.StyleContext.add_provider_for_display was called per TextPageView (sheet zoom), per window (pen swatches + notes font) and per present bar, with no remove anywhere — every closed tab/window leaked a display-wide provider in the long-running single instance. New _display_provider_while_realized() ties add/remove to realize/unrealize, which survives tab drags between windows (unrealize→realize on the new toplevel) and drops the provider on destroy; it returns its state dict so tests can observe the lifecycle. Tests: TestLassoSelect (straight-line-through-middle + _segments_intersect table), TestFollowNoteLink.test_follow_survives_current_file_deleted, TestTextFirstMode.test_zoom_css_provider_released_on_tab_close.

column2: Fast test tier: ./run_tests.sh --fast
column5: DONE (2026-07): the full suite takes ~1 minute under Weston (and grows with every feature), which made it the feedback-loop bottleneck for iterative work (fix → verify → next). conftest.py auto-marks a test class as 'window' when its source references PDFEditorWindow / PresenterWindow / Adw.Application (inspect.getsource + regex; no hand-maintained list to rot); ./run_tests.sh --fast runs -m 'not window' — the ~70 pure-logic/widget tests finish in ~3 s. Auto-marking cannot break correctness: everything still runs under the headless Weston compositor, so a misclassified test still passes — it only lands in the wrong SPEED tier. CI (python3 test_pdfeditor.py, no pytest markers involved) and the plain full run are unchanged. Documented workflow in CLAUDE.md: --fast (or the tests for the touched area) after every change, the full suite once before committing.

column2: Lasso resize (corner handles) and duplicate (Ctrl+D)
column5: DONE (2026-07): the lasso selection grew two GoodNotes-style verbs. RESIZE: the dashed selection box now draws 8x8 corner handles; pressing one (hit box matches the drawn handle, 4 px — so a grab just inside the box still means move even on tiny selections, _lasso_handle_at) scales the selection UNIFORMLY around the opposite corner, live during the drag, clamped to 0.05-20x. Stroke width scales WITH the factor — decision: a resized sketch should keep its look (a shrunk diagram with fat strokes reads wrong); revisit as a pen-settings toggle if it ever bothers. One undo entry ('lasso_scale', page, refs, f, ax, ay — undone by applying 1/f around the same anchor, so repeated undo/redo only accumulates float dust). DUPLICATE: Ctrl+D with a selection (canvas focus; the notes editor keeps its line-duplicate) clones the strokes offset by 14 screen px and selects the CLONES, so drag-to-place is the natural next gesture; the clones share a draw group = one undo removes them all (duplicate_selected). Tests: TestLassoSelect resize/handle-priority/duplicate cases.

column2: Text-page zoom parity: pinch zoom + Shift+click fit
column5: DONE (2026-07): first slice of the text/PDF interaction-parity push (#106). TextPageView gained (a) two-finger pinch zoom (Gtk.GestureZoom, capture phase on the overlay; anchors the gesture centre by scaling the scroll adjustments by z'/z — the sheet scales linearly so the wrap is identical — re-applied in an idle because the relayout is async) and (b) Shift+click fit-width while a DRAWING tool is active (fit_width(); with the text tool Shift+click keeps its standard extend-selection meaning, which is why fit is gated on the ink overlay being targetable). _on_ink_begin routes the Shift press to fit and flags the rest of the drag a no-op (_shift_fitting) so no dot is drawn and the eraser doesn't fire. Tests: TestTextFirstMode.test_pinch_zoom_scales_sheet / test_shift_click_fits_paper_to_window.

column2: Text/PDF tool parity (unified tools & shortcuts)
column5: Goal: the same tool set doing the same thing with the same shortcuts in both modes, so switching modes costs zero relearning. Gap audit (2026-07, after #105 closed pinch+fit; items 1-3 DONE 2026-07 via #108 on the #107 mode framework): (1) DONE LASSO on text pages — select/move/resize/duplicate/recolour/delete with re-anchoring (#95, #108). (2) DONE STRAIGHT-LINE SNAP — same rest-timer recipe as the PDF pen (#108). (3) DONE STROKE SMOOTHING on commit — shared PDFCanvas._smooth_points (#108). (4) DONE (2026-07) Ctrl+drag / middle-drag PAN on the text sheet — a capture-phase GestureDrag on the TextPageView overlay (button 0) claims only the two pan chords (middle-drag, or Ctrl+left via _pan_chord) and denies itself otherwise, so caret/pen/text-selection keep every other sequence; it grab-scrolls the ScrolledWindow adjustments (offset moves opposite the drag delta, content follows the pointer) and shows a grabbing cursor for the drag. Mirrors the PDF canvas' _panning; the README's generic 'Ctrl+drag / Middle-drag → Pan' row already covered it. FOLLOW-UP (user asked for full toolbar parity): pan is now ALSO a toolbar TOOL in text mode (_MODE_CHROME _mode_pan -> (pdf,text)); TextPageView.set_tool accepts 'pan' and _pan_chord returns True for a plain left-drag while tool=='pan' (so the pan tool rides the same capture gesture — no overlay target needed, just a grab cursor). Plus device parity: the MX Master thumb button (btn 10, via an EventControllerLegacy + motion controller — _on_thumb_event/_on_sheet_motion) grab-pans while held, and two-finger touchscreen drag now PANS as well as zooms (the pinch handler follows the live centroid: base scroll+centroid scale by f, minus the current centroid, so constant-distance two-finger drag translates). Tests: test_ctrl_and_middle_drag_pan_the_sheet (now covers the pan tool), test_thumb_button_pans_the_sheet, test_two_finger_drag_pans_and_zooms, test_pdf_only_chrome_is_hidden (pan+zoom now visible). (5) DONE (2026-07) Shift+drag ZOOM-TO-REGION on the text sheet — Shift now starts a dashed rubber-band in _on_ink_begin (was: fit-width at press); _on_ink_end zooms to the rect if it grew past 8x8 px (_zoom_to_region) else falls back to fit_width (so Shift+CLICK still fits — #105). _zoom_to_region is the text twin of the PDF _execute_zoom_to_rect: fit both axes (min(vw/rw, vh/rh)*0.97, clamped to the sheet zoom range), then set the scroll adjustments so the region lands centred — the sheet scales linearly (identical wrap) so the region keeps its shape; the marquee draws in _draw_lasso and the async relayout is re-applied via idle_add (same pattern as pinch). Only fires while a drawing/zoom tool targets the ink overlay; with the text tool Shift+drag keeps its extend-selection meaning. CONSISTENCY FIX (user feedback): the PDF bar exposes zoom-to-region as a TOOLBAR TOOL (the modifier-free twin of the Shift gesture), so the text bar now does too — _MODE_CHROME _mode_zoom -> (pdf, text), and TextPageView.set_tool accepts 'zoom' (claims the overlay like the pens); a plain drag with the tool selected runs the same marquee (self.tool=='zoom' OR Shift in _on_ink_begin). (Pan later also got a toolbar tool — see item 4 — after the user asked for full toolbar parity; the earlier 'pan stays gesture-only' call was reversed.) README's 'Shift+drag to zoom to region' + the tool-strip bullet already cover it. Tests: test_shift_drag_zooms_to_region, test_zoom_tool_drags_a_region_without_shift, fit-click fallback, and test_pdf_only_chrome_is_hidden (zoom now in the visible set). (6) DONE (2026-07) Ctrl+Shift+drag TEMP-HIGHLIGHTER on text pages — a capture-phase primary GestureDrag on the overlay (_on_temp_hl_begin/update/end, same escape pattern as Alt+drag) claims only Ctrl+Shift+left, borrows the highlighter for one stroke (sets self.tool='highlighter' so pen_style/_commit_stroke use it) and restores the tool on release; works over any tool incl. the caret since it is capture-phase above the TextView. Freed the chord from the pan gesture (_pan_chord returns False for Ctrl+Shift) and from the zoom branch (_on_ink_begin now zooms on Shift-only, not Ctrl+Shift). Ctrl+H toggle already routed (see #113). Test: TestTextFirstMode.test_ctrl_shift_drag_temp_highlighter. (7) PRESENTER VIEW (F5) + SHARE-TO-PHONE for text pages — WON'T DO (user 2026-07: not needed for text mode). PDF-only; would need a sheet-region renderer if ever revisited. Architecture note: do this as ONE tool enum + per-mode capability mask rather than per-mode reimplementations; the deck branch's _MODE_CHROME/doc_mode mode framework is now ON master (#107, ported 2026-07 so parity needn't wait for a deck-merge decision) — items 1-3 should land as _MODE_CHROME entries / per-mode capabilities on that framework rather than as new per-mode branches.

column2: Port the deck branch doc_mode mode framework to master
column5: DONE (2026-07): the doc_mode/_MODE_CHROME refactor from the experimental deck branch now lives on master, WITHOUT any deck feature code. Motivation: whether deck ever merges is undecided, but #106 (tool parity) needs the mode framework either way — porting it decouples parity work from the deck decision, and if deck does merge later, its biggest structural change is already on master so the merge shrinks to mostly-additive deck.py. What changed: DocumentSession.doc_mode (str, currently pdf|text) replaces the _text_mode boolean; _text_mode stays as a compatibility property (setter maps True->text, False->pdf-if-text) so the ~30 call sites are untouched and session STATE proxying keeps working. _update_header_for_mode now loops the _MODE_CHROME class table {widget name: modes tuple} — tool buttons (_mode_*) drive their pen-popover twins (_pmode_*) automatically — and shows each burger-menu group only in its mode; _apply_collapse_level gates presenter/share through the same table. Behaviour-preserving BY DESIGN: the table entries were copied from what master previously hid/showed, NOT from deck (deck e.g. shows the pan tool on text pages — master does not, yet; flipping a tuple in the table is now the whole change when parity items land). Deck-merge note: the port used deck code verbatim where possible so the eventual merge conflicts minimally; deck adds a deck mode to the same table plus _enter/_leave_deck_mode. Tests: TestDocMode (property round-trip, table sanity) + TestTextFirstMode.test_header_chrome_follows_mode_table (chrome matches the table in both directions).

column2: Text-page lasso + straight-line snap + stroke smoothing (parity items 1-3)
column5: DONE (2026-07): the top three gaps from the #106 audit, built on the #107 mode framework (_MODE_CHROME._mode_lasso became (pdf, text) — one tuple). LASSO (closes #95): TextPageView reuses PDFCanvas geometry verbatim (_point_in_polygon / _polyline_crosses_polygon so 2-point snapped lines are caught by crossing, _bbox_corners) but hit-tests in OVERLAY space — a stroke position already follows its mark+font, so selections stay honest when text edits move paragraphs. Verbs match the PDF: drag inside the dashed box moves, corner handles resize uniformly around the opposite corner (width scales with the factor, #104 decision), Ctrl+D duplicates offset 14px and selects the clones, Delete/Escape work, and the pen popover recolours the selection. The text twist (per #95): move/resize RE-ANCHORS via _reanchor_selected — fresh left-gravity mark at the transformed first point, offsets recomputed at the current font_px; old marks are kept undeleted so the new (anchor, entries) undo op can swap the before/after state back (same convention as erase). Stored-width algebra: drawn = width x font_now/font_stored, so re-anchor/duplicate/recolour rescale the stored width to keep the on-screen width intact (recolour stores width x font_stored/font_now so the CHOSEN width is what you see). Keyboard: a CAPTURE-phase EventControllerKey on the TextPageView (gated on tool==lasso AND a selection) beats the sheet editor own Delete/Ctrl+D since focus usually sits in the TextView; the window-level handler is the fallback for focus elsewhere. SNAP + SMOOTHING: same recipes as the PDF canvas, applied in _on_ink_end (not _commit_stroke, so tests/export calling _commit_stroke directly are untouched): rest timer (PDFCanvas.STRAIGHT_HOLD_MS) collapses the stroke to [start, cursor], further motion moves only the endpoint; freehand commits run PDFCanvas._smooth_points with the shared pen-popover strength (tp.get_smoothing -> session canvas.smoothing, like pen_style). Alt+drag quick-pen gets both for free (same handlers). Tests: TestTextPageLasso (8: select+crossing, move+undo re-anchor, resize width/pts, duplicate/delete, recolour, tool-switch clears, snap, smoothing). Remaining parity: #106 items 4-7.

column2: Finer Ctrl+Z granularity in the text editor (shorter undo steps)
column5: DONE (2026-07). Root cause found (was NOT GtkSourceView coalescing — that is already word-level; buf.undo() probes all showed 5 words -> 5 steps). Ctrl+Z is intercepted by the WINDOW-level unified canvas+notes timeline (#29, _on_undo_key -> _global_undo over _undo_timeline), NOT the buffer's native undo. A notes 'burst' spanned ALL typing until the next canvas action / page switch / save — so a whole paragraph was ONE entry, wiping everything. FIX: _on_notes_changed now ends the burst at a word/line boundary (_at_word_boundary: char just typed is space/tab/newline), so one word (with its trailing space) is one timeline entry; the next char opens a fresh burst. The #29 unification is preserved — the timeline still interleaves canvas strokes and notes edits chronologically, there are just more, smaller notes entries between strokes (user reminded us to keep the unify-drawing-and-text idea; we did). Redo peels back symmetrically. Verified the disagreement between my probes and the user: probes tested native buf.undo() (word-level), the app uses _global_undo (was session-level). Applies to both the notes panel and text-first sheet (same MarkdownNotesView + same window handler). Possible future refinement: also close a burst after an idle pause (timer) for long space-less runs. Tests: TestGlobalUndo.test_typing_undoes_word_by_word (replaces test_typing_burst_undone_as_one); draw/type/draw interleave + canvas-splits-bursts tests still green (they type single words). ORIGINAL: RANK 2 (do after 110). Ctrl+Z in the notes editor and text-first sheet reverts too much at once — GtkSourceView/GtkTextView coalesces a long run of typing into one undo step, so one Ctrl+Z can wipe a whole sentence/paragraph. Want shorter intervals: insert undo barriers so each Ctrl+Z peels back a smaller chunk. Approach: GtkTextBuffer has begin/end_user_action; the source view groups edits between them. Add barriers at natural boundaries — sentence-enders/newlines, a short idle pause after typing stops, and maybe a max run length — via a buffer 'insert-text'/'delete-range' hook that ends the current user-action group. Applies to both MarkdownNotesView (panel) and the text-first sheet (same widget). Care: our display-only Markdown re-rendering mutates tags, not text, so it should not create undo entries (verify irreversible/tag changes stay out of history — cf. begin_irreversible_action used on page load). Test: type across boundaries, assert N Ctrl+Z steps peel back the expected pieces. No README.

column2: Window reuse (open launched files as tabs) misbehaves — verify + fix
column5: RESOLVED 2026-07 — NOT A BUG. Reproduced off-screen against the headless test compositor: two sequential launches with the same app id forward + reuse correctly (the second prints 'opened X in the current window'). Launcher (~/.local/bin/sidemark -> /usr/bin/python3 ~/.local/share/sidemark/sidemark.py) and .desktop (Exec=sidemark %f) both resolve to an _INSTALLED_PATHS entry, so the installed copy uses the base id de.hspitz.sidemark and every Firefox/file-manager launch shares one instance. The earlier 'too many windows' was almost certainly the DEV checkout (./sidemark.py — a per-version .v<hash> id, row 97) running at the same time as the installed copy: two different ids never share, by design. On the installed copy alone it works; user confirmed 'now it works for me'. No code change. Original investigation notes follow. RANK 1. The feature EXISTS (rows 98 & 106): PDFEditorApp._open_target -> _reuse_target (get_active_window, focus order) opens a launched file as a tab in the last-used window instead of a new one; SIDEMARK_NEW_WINDOW forces a fresh window. User reports it still spawns too many windows when opening several files (e.g. Firefox auto-open). Needs a REPRO first, then a fix. Suspects: (a) get_active_window() returns None / a non-editor window when the forwarded command-line is handled, so it falls through to a new window; (b) the dev-vs-installed per-version app id (row 97) means a working-tree build does NOT forward into the installed instance — but Firefox uses the installed .desktop, so all real launches should share de.hspitz.sidemark and forward — confirm the installed copy is actually single-instance; (c) Wayland multi-monitor/workspace: the reused window is on another workspace so present() feels like nothing happened; (d) timing — command-line handled before focus settles. Instrument do_command_line's shell note (already logs reuse vs new) and the logger to confirm which branch fires. Test: simulate two _open_target calls, assert the second reuses. Ties into rows 98/106.

column2: Duplicate-download dialog (Firefox foo-1.pdf already have foo.pdf)
column5: RANK 4 (do last — niche + destructive). Firefox auto-opens downloads in Sidemark and renames re-downloads to foo(1).pdf / foo-1.pdf. When opening such a file, detect a likely-original sibling in the same folder (strip a trailing ' (N)' / '-N' / '_N' suffix -> candidate base name) and, if it exists AND looks like the same file (size + content hash, cheap for a few MB), offer a dialog: [Open the original instead & delete this copy] / [Keep both / open this one] / [Cancel]. Deleting is destructive -> confirmation required, never auto-delete; also handle the original being currently open in a tab (focus it). Other workflow ideas to weigh with the user: (1) don't delete, just OFFER to open the already-open/original instead (safer MVP); (2) a general content-hash de-dupe so opening any byte-identical file focuses the existing tab (cross-window dedup is already a deferred polish in the linking roadmap); (3) tell Firefox to overwrite instead (out of our control). Suggest starting with the non-destructive 'open the existing one instead' toast/dialog. Test: suffix->base heuristic table + same-content detection.

column2: Per-document width for text-first sheets
column5: DONE (2026-07). TextPageView.PAGE_WIDTH is now an instance value self.page_width (default 794); _apply_zoom/__init__/fit_width read it, so changing it re-wraps the sheet (mark+font-anchored ink rides along, as designed). set_page_width(px) clamps to [PAGE_WIDTH_MIN=360, PAGE_WIDTH_MAX=1400], re-lays out, and fires on_ink_changed (-> window _mark_dirty) so it persists. PERSISTENCE: ink_to_json writes 'page_width', load_ink restores it (set directly, not via the setter, so opening a doc does not dirty it); missing key falls back to the default. Export auto-follows (page_break_offsets / _write_text_pdf read the live view width). UI: DRAG THE PAPER SIDE EDGE — a capture-phase primary GestureDrag on the overlay claims only an unmodified left-drag that starts within EDGE_GRAB=7px of the paper's left/right border while the caret tool is active (denies otherwise, so typing/drawing/pan-Ctrl/zoom-Shift keep their drags); the grabbed edge follows the cursor and the centred sheet's width = 2x distance-from-centre / zoom. Hover over the edge shows an ew-resize cursor (_on_sheet_motion, caret tool only). RANK 3. Original notes: TextPageView.PAGE_WIDTH is a constant (794, ~A4@96dpi). User wants a per-document sheet WIDTH so a note used on half the screen isn't forced to the wide default. Distinct from zoom (row 91/#105, which scales paper+text+ink together, per-view, not persisted): width changes the wrap column. Approach: make PAGE_WIDTH an instance value with a setter that re-runs _apply_zoom (width feeds set_size_request and the ink is mark+font anchored, so it re-wraps cleanly); expose via a small control (header spin/slider or Ctrl+drag the paper edge) and PERSIST per document. Storage: the <name>-ink.json sidecar already carries meta for the page (autosave meta.json exists too) — add a 'page_width' key there so it is remembered without touching the .md text. Default stays 794. Care: changing width re-wraps text so mark-anchored strokes shift — that is expected (they ride the paragraph), but verify export (page_break_offsets / _write_text_pdf use PAGE_WIDTH) reads the per-doc width. Test: set width, assert wrap/size_request and round-trip through the sidecar.

column2: Unify text-mode gesture/modifier workflow (when do shortcuts apply)
column5: CLOSED 2026-07. Audit of the OPEN items: Ctrl+H is NOT mode-gated (_on_key line ~10609 -> _toggle_highlighter toggles the shared _mode_hl/_mode_pen buttons, which route to _text_page.set_tool in text mode) so it already toggles the sheet's highlighter; the lasso keyboard verbs (Delete/Esc/Ctrl+D) already act on the sheet — the sheet's own capture-phase _on_lasso_key wins when focus is in it (#108), and the window-level fallback (_on_key ~10659) targets the text page when _text_mode + has_lasso_selection. So no code change was needed; locked with TestTextFirstMode.test_ctrl_h_and_lasso_verbs_route_to_text_page. Model documented in the README shortcuts table (one row: Alt/Alt+right ink escape + the pen/hl/eraser/lasso/pan/zoom tools and Ctrl/middle/Shift/Ctrl+H gestures all match the PDF canvas). Remaining Ctrl+Shift+drag temp-highlighter GESTURE tracked under #106 item 6 (done separately). User feedback: in text-first mode the shortcuts feel inconsistent because what works depends on the selected tool (with the caret, Shift is text-select not zoom, etc.). Agreed mental model being converged on: the ACTIVE TOOL owns un-modified input; MODIFIERS add global gestures on top — Alt = quick ink (Alt+left pen, Alt+right eraser), Ctrl+drag / middle-drag = pan — and these work in EVERY tool including the caret; the one intentional exception is the caret tool reserving plain keys + Shift for typing/selection (user explicitly OK with that). DONE so far: Alt+right-drag now erases while the caret is active (was Alt+left-only pen) — _on_alt_begin claims Alt+left(pen)/Alt+right(eraser), denies Alt+middle so pan keeps it; mirrors the ink tools' left-draw/right-erase pairing. Tests: TestTextFirstMode.test_alt_right_drag_erases_in_text_tool. DECISIONS (user): Shift-zoom stays ink-tools/zoom-tool only — NO Ctrl+Shift combo under the caret (Shift keeps text-select while typing). NAMING/TOOLTIP FIX (user flagged): the Alt-hold ink hint used to sit on the CARET tooltip ('Alt+drag draws with the pen') while the pen tooltip said just 'Pen' — confusing (hint on the cursor, not the tool). Now the pen/eraser tooltips carry the hint per-mode (set in _update_header_for_mode: text -> 'Pen — or hold Alt while typing to draw' / 'Eraser (right-drag · Alt+right while typing)'; pdf -> plain), the caret tooltip is just 'click to place the caret and type', and it is the SAME 'Pen' in both modes (dropped the 'quick pen' wording). WHY Alt+Shift+click fits width (the 'zoom back to normal' combo the user likes — keep it): it is emergent, not special-cased — Alt routes the caret to the ink path (_on_alt_begin -> tool=pen), and the ink path treats Shift+click-with-no-rect as fit-width; composing the two rules yields fit-width without leaving the caret. That it falls out of the model is a good sign the model is consistent. OPEN: audit the ink tools' keyboard verbs (Delete/Esc/Ctrl+D) + Ctrl+H temp-highlighter on text pages (row 106 item 6); document the locked model in README.

column2: Unify the zoom-to-region rectangle across modes (look + free aspect + right-click cancel)
column5: DONE (2026-07), from user feedback that the zoom rectangle looked/behaved differently between PDF and text mode. THREE changes: (1) SHARED LOOK — extracted a module-level _draw_zoom_marquee(ctx,x,y,w,h,rgb) (translucent accent fill + dashed accent border) now called by BOTH PDFCanvas._draw and TextPageView._draw_lasso; before, the text side drew only a dashed outline (no fill) while the PDF side filled — so they looked different. Same accent already (both default to the theme green). (2) FREE-PROPORTIONED both — removed PDFCanvas._constrain_zoom_end (which snapped the PDF rect to the canvas aspect ratio); _on_drag_update now stores the raw cursor so the PDF rect follows the pointer exactly, like the text sheet. User explicitly prefers the free one for both. (3) RIGHT-CLICK CANCEL — a secondary-button GestureClick on each canvas (_on_secondary_pressed) aborts an in-progress zoom rect: PDF reuses the existing _ignoring 'dead drag' flag; the text page got a new _zoom_cancelled flag guarding _on_ink_update/_end (reset in _on_ink_begin) so the still-held left drag draws/zooms nothing. FIX (follow-up): the text rclick controller was attached to the parent Overlay, so during the ink drag pointer-grab it never fired — moved to self.ink (the drag-owning widget, like the PDF canvas) so cancel actually works in text mode; test guards the controller lives on ink. WHY THEY DIVERGED (user asked): the two modes are separate widgets (PDFCanvas DrawingArea in PDF-space vs TextPageView overlay in screen-space) and when text-mode zoom was added (row 106.5) the marquee + zoom math were reimplemented rather than shared — classic copy-then-tweak drift. The draw is now shared; the zoom-to-rect MATH is still twinned (_execute_zoom_to_rect vs _zoom_to_region) because the coordinate spaces/zoom models differ (PDF scale+offset vs sheet zoom+scroll) — a deeper unification (a shared zoom-region controller) is possible but bigger; logged as future work. Tests: TestZoomToRegion.test_zoom_rect_is_free_proportioned / test_right_click_cancels_zoom_rect, TestTextFirstMode.test_right_click_cancels_zoom_rect.

column2: One chord grammar across modes (modifiers x buttons pick the tool; discoverable highlights)
column5: DONE (2026-07). THE VISION (user): one modifier-chord grammar so tools rarely need toolbar clicks — modifiers x mouse buttons pick the tool per-gesture, identically in pdf and text mode, discoverable via the transient button highlight + tooltip hints. Three usage scenarios drive the design: mouse+keyboard (chords), keyboard+touch (left hand chords, finger draws), touch-only (PDF only: one finger draws, two fingers pan/zoom, toolbar switches — deliberately NO chord cleverness there). CENTERPIECE: module-level chord_tool(ctrl,shift,alt,mode,ink_active) is now THE grammar — gesture routing hints, the transient highlight and (by policy) tooltips all derive from it; never grow a second mapping. The table: Ctrl+Shift+Alt=lasso, Ctrl+Alt=anchor (pdf only), Ctrl+Shift=temp-highlighter, Ctrl=pan, Alt=THE INK<->TEXT FLIP (pdf: select-text; text: pen — the one deliberate asymmetry: Alt escapes to the OTHER mode's home tool), Shift=zoom-region (pdf, or text with an ink tool active). Shift-alone is NEVER load-bearing cross-mode (owned by text selection under the caret); Shift+Alt deliberately unassigned in BOTH modes (was select on pdf — now strict, swallowed via _ignoring so it can be assigned later). BUTTONS = the hand: left=tool, right=eraser, middle=NAVIGATION (plain=pan, Shift+middle=zoom-region — THE portable zoom chord, works under the caret), thumb(10)=hold-pan+scroll-zoom. NEW BEHAVIOURS: (1) text pages gained the Ctrl+Shift+Alt lasso chord (capture GestureDrag borrows tool='lasso', restores RAW so the selection survives — set_tool would clear it); pdf chord-lasso now also moves/scales via the shared _lasso_press (before, the chord always started a fresh loop). (2) right-DRAG erases under the caret: capture gesture must claim at PRESS (TextView opens its context menu on press, a deferred claim races the popup), so a clean right-CLICK re-opens the menu itself via view.activate_action('menu.popup') after GTK-mirroring caret placement (place_cursor unless inside selection). VERIFY ON REAL MOUSE: menu position comes from the caret, not the pointer — if it feels wrong, this is the piece to revisit. (3) Shift+middle zoom-region both modes (_finish_zoom_region extracted on the text page). (4) transient highlight: window now receives RAW (ctrl,shift,alt) from the canvas key tracking and maps via chord_tool per active-tab mode — the old text-mode crippling (only Alt->pen) is gone; PLUS button gestures light their button too (on_gesture_tool callback from both views -> _highlight_gesture_tool; release falls back to the still-held chord). (5) TOUCH: chord routing merges the window-tracked held-modifier booleans into gesture state (_chord_state on both views; text page reads the session canvas' flags via get_held_mods) because Wayland touch sequences carry no keyboard modifiers — this is what makes the keyboard+touch scenario work; UNVERIFIED on real touch hardware (no touchscreen at dev machine). Guard: reset_modifiers() on window notify::is-active=False, else a modifier released while unfocused would misroute every later drag (stale-held bug). Tooltips + the ? shortcuts popover updated (Shift+middle zoom, right-drag-under-caret, chord lasso). Tests: TestToolModes chord grammar/touch/reset, TestZoomToRegion Shift+middle, TestTextPageLasso chord-lasso/rerase/mzoom + guard exclusivity, gesture-highlight window test. MANUAL CHECKS still owed (no wtype/ydotool for injection): right-click menu re-pop feel, Shift+middle on a real mouse, keyboard+touch chords. FOLLOW-UP (same July pass, user feedback): the THUMB button (10) is the ergonomic wheel-button stand-in — everywhere the middle button navigates, the thumb must too (and wheel-press still works for mice without one). Shift+thumb-hold now rubber-bands zoom-to-region in BOTH modes (_thumb_zooming flag; PDF updates the rect in _on_motion, text in _on_sheet_motion; both commit via a _finish_zoom_region() extracted on the PDF canvas to mirror the text page's). Scroll-while-thumb-held now also zooms the TEXT sheet (capture EventControllerScroll -> zoom_step, rebasing the pan origin so the next motion doesn't jump; PDF had this via _on_scroll). Zoom tooltip is now mode-swapped in _update_header_for_mode: the text variant does NOT advertise plain Shift+drag (caret owns Shift). DEAD-CODE SWEEP (user asked): AST scan of zero-reference defs found the codebase clean — do_GET/log_message/do_activate are framework-called, can_nav_back is tested API; deleted _write_autosave (unused wrapper) and _modifier_tool (superseded by chord_tool; tests repointed). FOUND: zoom_back() — the _zoom_stack pop that every zoom-to-region pushes onto — is tested but wired to NO UI; decide whether to bind it (Escape? a chord?) or drop the stack. FOLLOW-UP 2 (user feedback): text-sheet scroll-zoom now zooms TOWARD THE CURSOR, not the top-left — zoom_step_at(direction, vx, vy) mirrors the PDF canvas' _zoom_at: the sheet scales uniformly about the content origin, so a content point (scroll+v) scales by f and we solve the scroll that keeps it under v (same math as the pinch), re-applying on GLib.idle for the async relayout; returns the new scroll so a live thumb-pan rebases without a jump. Wired to BOTH thumb-scroll and Ctrl+scroll (font_zoom_cb -> zoom_step_at at tp._mouse_xy; Ctrl+=/-/0 anchor to the last pointer pos, top-left when the mouse never entered). Tooltips audited against chord_tool this pass — all match (zoom tooltip already mode-swapped). Test: test_scroll_zoom_anchors_to_cursor. FOLLOW-UP 3 (user-reported, 2026-07): three 'the handler is fine, the event never arrives' bugs — the lesson is to test the PATH, not the handler (all of these passed in isolation while being dead in the app). (a) Ctrl+scroll NEVER zoomed the text sheet, and the notes panel's Ctrl+scroll font zoom was equally dead: GtkScrolledWindow installs its OWN capture-phase scroll controller (BOTH_AXES|KINETIC) in its init and returns STOP whenever it can scroll, while both zoom handlers were BUBBLE-phase on the view inside it. So both only worked while the content was too short to scroll and silently died once it had any — which is why it survived so long. Fix: capture-phase controller on an ANCESTOR of the ScrolledWindow (TextPageView._on_sheet_scroll for the sheet; MarkdownNotesView.attach_zoom_scroll(ancestor) for the panel, which the owner must call with _notes_box). The ScrolledWindow itself is NOT a valid host: same widget + same phase run in add order and GTK's is added first. (b) plain scrolling on the sheet worked with the caret but not with a drawing tool: set_tool -> ink.set_can_target(True) makes the ink overlay the event TARGET, which cuts the ScrolledWindow out of the propagation path entirely, so GTK's own scrolling never runs. Fix: _on_sheet_scroll now owns ALL sheet scroll (zoom AND pan, panning via the adjustments with PDFCanvas.WHEEL_PAN_STEP / 1px-per-SURFACE-unit), so scroll is identical whatever the tool — same shape as PDFCanvas._on_scroll, which also hand-rolls its scroll-pan. (c) Ctrl+R did nothing in text mode — and NOT for a routing reason (the probe showed the sheet does not swallow it and it reached _on_key fine): _reload() read only _path, which is the PDF, and a text-first page's document is the .md in _notes_path, so it hit 'if not self._path: return'. Fix: _path or _notes_path, and only pass --page for a real PDF. AUDIT of every other bare _path guard: _on_export and _ocr_current are correct — PDF-only by design AND they fail loudly ('No PDF is open'); _reload was the only silent no-op. Shortcut phases audited too and are sound: app-level keys that must beat a focused editor already live in capture controllers (_on_undo_key for Ctrl+Z/Y, _on_global_key for Ctrl+W/Ctrl+Shift+T/Ctrl+backslash/PageUp/PageDown, the sheet's own for the lasso verbs); _on_key is bubble and correctly loses Ctrl+C, Delete and the arrows to the editor. Rules recorded in CLAUDE.md so the next shortcut lands in the right phase by construction. AMENDMENT (user, 2026-07): Shift+Alt is NO LONGER unassigned — it is now 'zoom' in BOTH modes, and it is THE portable KEYBOARD zoom chord. The user found the hole by reasoning from the grammar itself: Alt is documented as the ink<->text flip, so Alt+Shift should read as 'Shift while drawing' = zoom-to-region. That composition is exactly right and the table now says so (the alt+shift branch sits above the alt-only and shift-only branches). This closes a real gap: under the caret Shift alone is text selection, so before this there was NO keyboard route to zoom-to-region at all — only Shift+middle-drag, which needs a mouse with a wheel button. Routing: PDFCanvas' drag-begin previously matched Shift AND NOT Alt then swallowed Alt+Shift via _ignoring; it now simply matches Shift (Alt+Shift lands in the same zoom branch). On the text page the ink overlay is not targetable while the caret owns the sheet, so _on_ink_begin can never see these drags — _on_alt_begin (the caret's capture-phase escape gesture) is the only route, and it used to DENY whenever Shift was held; it now borrows tool='zoom' for Alt+Shift+left and lets the shared _on_ink_begin/_update/_end run the rubber-band (so Alt+Shift+click falls back to fit-width, like Shift+click). NOTE the tooltip WAS already mode-swapped correctly and did not advertise plain Shift+drag on a text page, but the ? shortcuts popover DID list 'Shift+Drag → Zoom to region' unconditionally, which is false under the caret — now labelled PDF-only, with Alt+Shift+Drag listed beside it. Tooltips name the chord that actually works per mode. ZOOM CEILING raised again to 16.0 (user) — see the range note above. ZOOM_BACK IS NOW WIRED (user, closing row 115's open decision): Escape steps back out of the last zoom-to-region. It had a _zoom_stack that every zoom-to-region pushed onto, and a tested zoom_back(), but NO UI binding — the stack was being maintained for a feature you could not reach. The workflow it buys: dive into a figure, then a detail of it, and step back out through the views you came from instead of fitting the page and hunting for your place. PARITY: zoom_back was PDF-only, so binding a key to it would have opened a fresh pdf/text gap — the sheet now has the same stack, storing (zoom, scroll_h, scroll_v) where the canvas stores (scale, offset_x, offset_y), and fit_width() clears the history just as zoom_to_fit() does (fitting is a fresh start). PRECEDENCE on a contended key: Escape is claimed first by the [[ link autocomplete popup (the view's own controller, at the target, so it wins), then by a lasso selection (dropping a selection is the more immediate 'cancel'), and only then by zoom_back — which claims it ONLY when can_zoom_back() says there is somewhere to go, so Escape stays free for anything else. Bubble phase is correct here (unlike Ctrl+Z etc.): GtkTextView has no Escape binding, so the focused editor does not swallow it. can_zoom_back() added to both surfaces for that guard. MANUAL CHECKS NOW DONE (user, 2026-07): verified on real hardware and 'feels good' — right-click menu re-pop on text pages, Shift+middle on a real mouse, keyboard+touch chords, plus the later additions (Alt+Shift+drag zoom under the caret, Escape stepping back out of a zoom, 16x sheet zoom, and the document-width pen when zooming in to write small). Nothing owed on this row any more.

column2: PDF/text parity audit: shared zoom+erase policy, segment eraser, touchpad zoom
column5: DONE (2026-07). A pdf/text PARITY AUDIT plus the bugs it found. METHOD (repeatable): the two canvases (PDFCanvas, TextPageView) are parallel implementations of the same gestures, so they drift. Walk the chord_tool grammar x {pdf,text}; for each cell find BOTH handlers and compare four things — sign, magnitude, ANCHOR, clamping; diff duplicated constants; and ask 'is there a test asserting BOTH sides?' (most gesture tests only drive one). RESULT, and it validated the dedup thesis sharply: every behaviour where the sheet reuses a PDFCanvas static/pure helper held parity (_smooth_points, STRAIGHT_HOLD_MS, _point_in_polygon/_polyline_crosses_polygon, the shared _draw_zoom_rect); the ONE place it reimplemented — the eraser hit-test — was the one place with a live bug. FOUND+FIXED: (1) ERASER could not erase a straight line on a text page: _erase_at tested only stored VERTICES, but _snap_to_straight collapses a stroke to [first,last], so a snapped line had nothing to hit in its middle and only erased near its ends (the PDF side always tested segment distance). Exactly the trap the lasso hit in row 102. Now shares PDFCanvas._stroke_hits. Radius model unified via a new module-level erase_radius(width) = width/2 + ERASE_SLACK_PX: the eraser deletes a whole stroke on contact, so the radius is only 'did I touch the ink' — it must track the stroke's own width because the distance is measured from the CENTRELINE (a flat small radius cannot erase the visible edge of a fat highlighter; a flat big one deletes thin ink from a distance). Dropped the old flat ERASE_RADIUS=9 floor per user feedback — no generous radius, just half the on-screen width + small slack, carrying the sheet's font scale. (2) TOUCHPAD zoom was chunky on the sheet and smooth on the PDF: the sheet ignored the scroll unit and applied a full wheel step per event. New shared zoom_factor_for_scroll(smooth, dy) is now the one table (proportional+clamped for SURFACE deltas, fixed notch for WHEEL). It also made wheel in/out exact inverses (was 1.1 in but 0.9 out on pdf, so a notch each way drifted). (3) Dedup of duplicated DECISIONS: ZOOM_RECT_MIN_PX (the 8px rubber-band threshold, hardcoded on both canvases), PDFCanvas.SCALE_MIN/MAX (0.1/20.0 was hardcoded twice inside PDFCanvas itself). Deliberately NOT deduped: the mechanics. A shared base class / viewport abstraction would be a false abstraction — one substrate is a scale-transform DrawingArea, the other a ScrolledWindow whose zoom is a CSS font-size change with an ASYNC reflow (which is why the anchored zoom needs a GLib.idle re-apply). Intentional divergences left alone: zoom range (0.1-20x pdf vs 0.5-3x text — font reflow), Shift+click fits page vs width, pan mechanics. FOLLOW-UP BUGS from the same audit, see row 115's routing notes: Ctrl+scroll and plain scroll on the sheet, and Ctrl+R in text mode. FOLLOW-UP (user, same pass): two more pdf/text divergences the audit's matrix missed — both about what a unit MEANS. (a) PEN WIDTH was screen-relative on the sheet and document-relative on the PDF canvas: PDF draws strokes inside a ctx scaled by self.scale so width is in PDF units (the live preview too), while the sheet drew the live stroke at set_line_width(width) unscaled and stored the raw pen width, so the render factor is exactly 1 at draw time — the pen was always the same SCREEN thickness and its DOCUMENT width was width/zoom. Consequences: at 3x, ink drawn at 1x renders 3x thick but new ink renders 1x, so new ink does not match the ink beside it; and anything drawn zoomed-in goes hairline when you zoom out. User chose PDF parity (document units — zoom is a pure magnifier, ink never depends on the zoom you drew at). Fix: _commit_stroke stores width*self.zoom and the live preview draws width*self.zoom. Strokes render at width*font_px/st['font_px'], so baking the draw-time zoom in makes that render as pen_width*zoom at every later zoom. NO migration risk: ink drawn at zoom 1.0 stores exactly the pen width, and the sheet could not zoom when existing sidecars were written. Test asserts the property that matters — two strokes drawn at 1x and 3x are the same on-screen width at every viewing zoom. (b) ZOOM CEILING: ZOOM_MIN/MAX was 0.5-3.0 and the user could not zoom close enough to write a small detail by hand. Raised to 0.25-8.0; the sheet zooms by scaling the font, so the old ceiling was conservative rather than technical (the real limit is how big a Pango layout stays pleasant). The audit had filed the 0.1-20x vs 0.5-3.0 range gap as 'intentional (font reflow)' — that was wrong, it was just untested.

column2: Test suite is flaky under full-run load (passes in isolation)
column5: Noted 2026-07, NOT investigated — parked deliberately for its own session. Symptom: a full ./run_tests.sh occasionally fails ONE test that passes both in isolation and on a clean tree, so it is not a regression. Seen twice in one session on DIFFERENT tests — TestPresenterMode::test_stack_peek_renders_next_page_not_on_last and TestShareToPhone::test_server_serves_only_the_token_path — which points at load/timing rather than either test's logic. Prime suspects: the share server binds a real port (collision or a race with another test's server), and _settle()-style main-loop pumping that is a fixed number of iterations rather than a wait-for-condition, so a slow full run under the headless compositor can outrun it. Reproduce by running the full suite repeatedly; a rerun plugin (pytest-rerunfailures) would only mask it. Worth fixing because a suite that cries wolf trains you to ignore it — which is exactly how a real regression slips through.

column2: Paste images from the clipboard (PDF pages and text-first pages)
column5: DESIGNED 2026-07 with the user; validated by experiment before any code. SCOPE: Ctrl+V pastes an image in BOTH modes; Ctrl+C copies a lasso selection (ink AND images). In-app copy/paste must round-trip the REAL objects (strokes stay editable vector strokes, images stay image objects) while other apps still get a picture. == MODEL == One shared object model (bytes + rect + crop + rotate) + one clipboard layer, used by both modes; the substrates differ, the decision does not (CLAUDE.md 'one table, not two'). == CLIPBOARD (both modes) == Copy publishes a Gdk.ContentProvider.new_union of (a) a private 'application/x-sidemark-objects+json' payload and (b) a Gdk.Texture. Paste does read_async([PRIVATE,'image/png']) so Sidemark prefers the private type (lossless, editable) and falls back to a texture from any other app. Verified end-to-end under the weston harness: wire offers BOTH mimes, in-app paste picks the private one, external paste yields the texture. TRAP (GTK/PyGObject, cost me the feature silently): Gdk.ContentProvider.new_for_value(tex) auto-boxes to the CONCRETE type GdkMemoryTexture, which has NO registered serializers -> the clipboard advertises NOTHING to other apps while still working perfectly in-app (GTK short-circuits to the in-process GType). Must box the BASE type: Gdk.ContentProvider.new_for_value(GObject.Value(Gdk.Texture, tex)) -> advertises image/png,tiff,jpeg,webp,bmp,avif. Headless tests and in-app clicking both PASS while external paste is broken, so assert on clipboard.get_formats().union_serialize_mime_types().contain_mime_type('image/png'), not on read_texture_async. == TEXT MODE (user's call: 'similar to the drawings, no markdown embed') == An image is another object in the -ink.json sidecar anchored to a GtkTextMark like a stroke, so it rides text edits and inherits the existing lasso select/move/resize/duplicate + undo for free. The .md stays byte-pure (test_save_keeps_md_pure_and_writes_sidecar). Rejected: a real ![](path) Markdown embed (Obsidian-compatible but needs GtkTextChildAnchor inline-image rendering in MarkdownNotesView, which is display-only Pango markup today). == PDF MODE (user's idea: 'add it on a separate layer') == The sidecar is the SOURCE OF TRUTH; the PDF optional content group (OCG) layer is a RENDER TARGET regenerated on save. Three load-bearing ingredients, each of which I watched fail without it: (1) OWNERSHIP via /OC. doc.add_ocg('Sidemark images', on=True) + insert_image(oc=ocg) puts /OC on our image; on reopen ours reads /OC=<xref>, the document's own figures read null. This is the ONLY per-image marker that survives a round-trip, and it is what makes reposition/crop/resize-after-reopen possible at all. on=True means every other viewer (Zathura/Firefox/Acrobat) renders it normally. (2) UNIQUIFY before insert. PyMuPDF DEDUPLICATES byte-identical images onto one xref; when that happens /OC is LOST on the first reopen and the placement becomes permanently unrecognizable AND unremovable (a stale ghost on the page forever). This is a REAL workflow, not a corner case: copy a figure out of the PDF and paste it back. Fix: inject a unique PNG tEXt chunk before insert_image so the input bytes differ. Note insert_image TRANSCODES to FlateDecode, so the chunk does not persist in the PDF - it only has to defeat insert-time dedup. Verified: without it ownership dies on cycle 0. (3) STRIP-AND-REGENERATE, never delete_image(). delete_image() only BLANKS the xref and leaves the dead object plus its 'Do' operator in the content stream -> every save/move cycle LEAKS a ghost placement (measured: 2->4->6->8->10 images, 2->6 placements, 4.5k->14k bytes over five nudges of ONE image). Instead: page.clean_contents(), find our names in Resources/XObject whose xref has our /OC, regex out each 'q...cm /Name Do Q' block, null the resource entry, update_stream. With this the file reaches a true STEADY STATE (2 imgs, 2 placements, constant bytes, document's own figure untouched, our image free to move). (4) PERF: re-place unchanged images with insert_image(xref=...) - re-places by reference with NO re-encode (0.005s vs a full compress). Only genuinely new pastes get encoded. Without it every Ctrl+S would re-compress the whole document. MEASURED at 100 heavy images (2MB each, incompressible, 20MB PDF): strip 0.02-0.04s + re-place 0.38s + write 0.04s = ~0.45s per save, placements pinned at 100, no leak. 100 images is a non-issue. Slow size creep (+13KB/save) with garbage=0; pick a compacting-save policy deliberately. TRAP (PyMuPDF): get_image_info(xrefs=True) and get_image_rects() LIE - they resolve placements by VISUAL match, not by name, and report the same xref for two different look-alike images. Do not build on them. Ground truth is the content stream ('/fzImg1 Do' + its cm matrix) plus the Resources dict ('/fzImg1 -> 10 0 R'). Rejected for PDF: bake-only (permanent after Ctrl+S, no undo/move - diverges from ink which stays editable forever); sidecar-only (invisible in every other PDF viewer); PDF-as-truth mutated in place (this is what leaks). Free rotation/crop are fields in the model applied when regenerating the layer, NOT destructive re-encodes - insert_image only takes rotate= in 90 steps and has no crop param, which is why the sidecar must be the truth. == LASSO COPY == NEW capability, not a fix: Ctrl+C today routes to PDFCanvas.copy_selection() (~sidemark.py:2083), which is TEXT copy gated on _selected_words; the lasso has no copy path, only Ctrl+D duplicate (~:6314). Rule: a lasso selection wins Ctrl+C, text selection keeps it otherwise - extend that one gate, don't add a second. == BUILD ORDER == shared model + clipboard -> text mode (lower risk, proves the model) -> PDF layer + save -> lasso copy. TESTS: the invariant every trap above violated is 'several save/reopen/move cycles keep placement count constant and leave the document's own images untouched' - drive that, plus the wire-format assertion, plus both modes (a pdf/text parity lesson from row 116). deck's paste_image() (read_texture_async/save_to_png_bytes) is the clipboard-read reference; its base64-in-JSON storage does NOT transfer (files stay plain). == BUILT 2026-07 (text mode; PDF mode still to come) == Shared clipboard layer (clipboard_content_for/paste_objects) + text-page images landed. IMAGES ARE PAINTED BY THE VIEW, NOT THE INK OVERLAY: MarkdownNotesView.do_snapshot_layer draws them in Gtk.TextViewLayer.BELOW_TEXT (buffer coords) via TextPageView._snapshot_images. The user asked for images UNDER the text (write over a picture, no words hidden) and that one change buys three things at once: text stays legible, and the PDF export - which rasterises tp.view through Gtk.WidgetPaintable in _write_text_pdf - picks images up FOR FREE. Drawing them on the ink overlay (the obvious first move, and what I did first) covers the words AND silently vanishes from the export, because the overlay is not the widget the export snapshots. Consequence to remember: an image mutation must queue_draw the VIEW (_images_changed), not just the ink - self.ink.queue_draw() alone leaves the picture stale. PASTE POSITION: NOT the caret. paste_point() = the mouse when it is over the sheet, else the middle of the view; the image is centred there and the offset is stored relative to its anchor mark so it still rides the paragraph. Reason (user): pasting must work with any tool, and with the pen/lasso in hand there is no useful caret. Ctrl+V is a CAPTURE controller on the overlay (beside the lasso verbs) and only CLAIMS the key when the clipboard holds something the editor cannot use; under the caret (tool == 'text') a text/plain payload wins, with any other tool the image wins - a browser copy carries both. BUG FIXED (user-reported, reload): _save_text_ink() gated the sidecar on `tp.strokes or os.path.exists(ink_file)`, so a page whose only content was a pasted image wrote NOTHING and lost the image on reload. An image is content too -> `tp.strokes or tp.images or ...`. The TextPageView-level round-trip test passed the whole time because it never went through that window-level gate; the regression test now drives _save_text_ink itself. STILL OPEN: PDF mode (the OCG layer, per the design above), Ctrl+C lasso copy, lasso select/move/resize of images (they render + persist but the lasso does not pick them up), crop/rotate UI (rotate is honoured in rendering + persistence already).

column2: Crop a pasted image (both modes)
column5: Split out of row 118 (2026-07-17, user: 'leave crop for later in an issue') once paste/move/resize/rotate/copy had landed for text pages. An image object already carries the field the renderer would need; nothing crops it. DESIGN (decided in 118, do not re-litigate): a crop is a FIELD ON THE MODEL applied when rendering, NOT a destructive re-encode of the stored PNG bytes - so it stays adjustable forever and repeated crops never degrade the picture. The stored bytes are the original. This matters most on the PDF side: insert_image has no crop parameter (and rotate only in 90 steps), which is exactly why the sidecar is the source of truth and the OCG layer is a regenerated render target. Do it ONCE for both modes: the image model and the draw path are shared (draw_image / TextPageView._snapshot_images), so a crop rect belongs next to 'rotate' and is honoured in both. UI: the natural gesture is a crop mode on the lasso selection (drag the edges inward), distinct from the corner handles which resize. Watch the same traps the rest of 118 hit: store the rect in DOCUMENT units (not the zoom you cropped at - row 116's pen-width lesson); go through _overlay_to_buffer_f, never the int-truncating helpers (that is what made rotation lumpy); and repaint via _images_changed() (the VIEW paints images, not the ink overlay). Ships with: the crop rect in ink_to_json/load_ink, one undo entry, and copy_selection carrying it.
